question: why soil is considered as a living system
title: Why Soil Is Considered a Living System: The Biology That Makes It Work | Scotty's Gardening Lab
metaDescription: Soil isn't just dirt — it's one of the most complex living systems on earth. Here's why scientists and regenerative growers consider soil a living entity, and why that understanding changes how you garden.
content:
Here's the thing about dirt that most people never learn. When you look at a handful of healthy soil, you are holding more living organisms than there are human beings on earth. Every gram of it. Every single gram.

A teaspoon of biologically rich garden soil contains up to one billion bacteria. Several yards of fungal filaments, coiled and compressed into a space you can hold in one hand. Thousands of protozoa. Scores of nematodes. All of that in a teaspoon of soil that looks, to most people, like simple brown dirt.

That is not an inactive mineral substrate. That is a living system — one of the most complex and least understood ecosystems on the planet, operating entirely beneath our feet.

Understanding why soil is considered a living system is not academic. It's the most practical foundation you can build as a gardener, a farmer, or any person who grows food. Once you understand that soil is alive, everything about how you treat it changes.

Soil is considered a living system because it contains billions of organisms per gram — bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes, arthropods, and earthworms — that interact in complex food web relationships, cycle nutrients, build soil structure, and regulate plant health. The USDA defines soil health as the capacity of soil to function as a vital living ecosystem. Remove the biology and you have a mineral substrate. Keep the biology healthy and you have the most productive growing medium on earth.

## The Organisms That Make Soil Alive

Let me walk you through the layers of life that make up what scientists call the soil food web. This is the community Scotty's talking about when he says the web of life that you need in your food starts in the soil.

**Bacteria** are the foundation. They are the most numerous organisms in soil by a massive margin — billions per gram. Different bacterial species specialize in different tasks: some fix atmospheric nitrogen into forms plants can use, some break down specific organic compounds, some produce compounds that suppress plant pathogens. Bacteria are the primary decomposers of fresh organic matter, the first organisms to colonize a new substrate and begin breaking it down.

**Fungi** are the second foundational layer, and in many ways the most structurally important. Fungal hyphae — the microscopic filaments that are the body of a fungus — physically bind soil particles together. The USDA's Soil Biology Primer documents how bacteria and earthworms produce a slime that aggregates soil particles, but fungal hyphae create a physical mesh that holds those aggregates in structure. Without fungi, soil crumbles. With them, it builds the aggregates — the irregular clumps of bound particles — that allow water and air to move through the soil profile.

Mycorrhizal fungi take the plant relationship one step further. These specialized fungi colonize plant roots and extend their hyphal networks far beyond what the roots themselves can reach. In exchange for carbohydrates from the plant, they deliver phosphorus, micronutrients, and water from across a much wider volume of soil. This is a biological internet — a trading network between plants and fungi that has been operating for an estimated 400 million years.

**Protozoa** eat bacteria. That predation relationship is critical to nutrient cycling. When protozoa consume bacteria, they release excess nitrogen — nitrogen that was locked inside bacterial cells — into the soil solution in plant-available form. Without protozoa doing this grazing work, bacterial nitrogen stays locked in microbial biomass and plants can't access it.

**Nematodes** are simple roundworms operating multiple roles in the soil food web simultaneously. Some nematodes eat bacteria. Some eat fungi. Some eat other nematodes. Some are plant parasites. The balance between beneficial and pathogenic nematode communities in a healthy soil food web self-regulates — predatory nematodes keep plant-parasitic ones in check. In depleted soil where the food web has collapsed, this self-regulation disappears and plant-parasitic nematodes can dominate.

**Earthworms** are the engineers. They mix and aggregate soil particles, creating channels that aerate the soil and provide pathways for root growth. Their castings — earthworm digestive byproducts — are nutrient-dense, biologically active, and one of the finest additions you can make to any garden bed. Earthworm populations in healthy soil can exceed one million per acre.

## The Soil as a Digestive System

Here's the concept that, once it clicked for me, changed how I think about gardening entirely. Your soil is not growing your plants. Your soil is digesting organic matter and releasing nutrients for your plants. The plants don't eat dirt. They drink a nutrient-rich solution produced by the biological activity in the soil.

Digestion is the right word. When you put organic matter — wood chips, compost, plant debris — on your soil, the microbial community begins to process it. First the bacteria and fungi colonize the surface. Then protozoa eat the bacteria. Then nematodes eat the protozoa. At every step of that food chain, nutrients are released in plant-available forms. The more complete and active the food web, the more efficiently organic matter is converted to plant nutrition.

A key concept in understanding the decay cycle is digestion. Your soil is a living entity and you are feeding it organic matter which it is breaking down. If you have a rough soil organic matter, like say a wood chip, you're going to see ants and things that cut and break. You'll see large beetles. As that gets digested and broken down into finer material, you'll see earthworms and small beetles and little crawly things. That entire progression — from large-particle organic matter down to fine, broken-down humus — is the digestive process of the soil.

## What Living Soil Looks Like: The Aggregate Test

You can see evidence of soil life without a microscope. The easiest way is to look for aggregates.

An aggregate is a clump of soil particles that have been bound together by microbial glue — the polysaccharides and glomalin that bacteria and fungi secrete. When you pick up a healthy aggregate, it holds together with mild pressure and breaks apart cleanly when you apply more force. When you hold it up to light, you can see that it's not a solid mass — it's a complex structure of particles with pore spaces between them.

Dead, compacted soil has no aggregates. The particles are loose and fall apart into powder or pack together without pore space. There's no structure because there's no biology secreting the compounds that create structure.

The USDA NRCS describes this aggregate structure as the physical signature of healthy soil. Aggregates create the pore spaces that allow water and air to move through the soil profile — water infiltration, root penetration, and gas exchange all depend on aggregate structure that only biology can build and maintain.

## Why This Matters Practically

I'm not telling you the soil is alive just because it's interesting, though it is genuinely fascinating. I'm telling you because it changes every practical decision you make as a grower.

**Tillage** physically destroys the fungal networks that build aggregate structure. Every time you till, you collapse a system that took months to build. No-till or minimal-till gardening is a biological decision, not just a labor-saving one.

**Synthetic fertilizers** suppress the food web by making the microbial intermediaries redundant. When plants can take up nutrients directly without fungal and bacterial assistance, they stop feeding the microbes. The microbial community shrinks. The soil biology degrades. You need more fertilizer next year because the biology that was doing the work for free is no longer there.

**Compost** does the opposite. It feeds the food web. Mature compost introduces hundreds of microbial species and the organic matter they need to colonize and spread through the soil.

**Bare soil** is a dead soil in the making. The biology in the top layers needs cover — mulch, living plants, or both — to maintain the moisture and temperature that allows it to thrive.

Albert Howard understood this a hundred years ago. He studied traditional Indian farming systems that maintained fertility without synthetic inputs and traced that fertility to the continuous feeding of soil biology through organic matter cycling. He wasn't describing a mystical connection. He was describing the soil food web before that vocabulary existed.

The biology of soil is the mechanism of agriculture. Feed it, protect it, and stop killing it — and it will grow food for you indefinitely. That's not a philosophy. That's just how soil works.

---

1. USDA NRCS — Soil Health: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/conservation-basics/natural-resource-concerns/soil/soil-health
2. USDA NRCS — Soil Biology Primer: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/resources/education-and-teaching-materials/soil-biology-primer
3. Piedmont Master Gardeners — The Soil Food Web: Nature's Way to Build Healthy Soils: https://www.piedmontmastergardeners.org/article/the-soil-food-web-natures-way-to-build-healthy-soils/
4. Nature Scitable — The Soil Biota: https://www.nature.com/scitable/knowledge/library/the-soil-biota-84078125/
5. SARE — Building Soils for Better Crops: The Living Soil: https://www.sare.org/publications/building-soils-for-better-crops/the-living-soil/

sources.title: The Soil Food Web: Nature's Way to Build Healthy Soils
Building Soils for Better Crops: The Living Soil

scottyQuotes: There is a life web within the soil. It starts with bacteria and acacia and funguses and all these very small organisms that are living within the soil, processing all the different calories and all the different minerals and vitamins to provide to the plants.
A key concept in understanding the decay cycle is digestion. Your soil is a living entity and you are feeding it organic matter which it is breaking down.

I get asked all the time: what is living soil? How can you tell if your soil is living? The easiest way is to find an aggregate. It's a bunch of random particles all bound together. When you crush it, it should just break apart. What's holding it together is microbial glue — that's life.
